aliases: Roasted Japanese green tea latte premix

english: Roasted Japanese green tea latte premix

region_of_origin: Japan (product format popularised in Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for instant 3-in-1 hojicha latte. FOODON_03301099 (tea beverage) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for instant premixed hojicha latte is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific code for instant premixed hojicha latte with creamer and sugar exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to instant 3-in-1 hojicha latte. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for commercial instant tea premix products.

allergens: milk derivatives (non-dairy creamer may contain casein)

serving.description: 1 sachet prepared in 150 ml hot water
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one sachet (approximately 20 g of powder) dissolved in 150 ml of hot water, yielding a ready-to-drink cup of approximately 170 ml.

nutrient_source.title: 3-in-1 Hojicha Latte — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 3-in-1 hojicha latte (cr_id D09020006). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the source record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for instant 3-in-1 hojicha latte. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the analogous food 'instant sweetened tea drink with creamer and sugar' from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reflects the dominant glycaemic driver being sucrose (GI ~65) in a liquid matrix. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 9.41 g × 0.20 kg serving fraction) / 100 ≈ (65 × 1.882) / 100 ≈ 1.22 × 10 = 12.2 per 20 g sachet serving dissolved in water. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.47 g sugar per 100 g; AHA 2018 evidence links SSB consumption to elevated blood pressure independently of sodium intake
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this product because its sugar content (6.47 g/100 g) classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which the AHA 2018 guidelines identify as independently raising blood pressure risk; plain unsweetened hojicha brewed from loose powder is a suitable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (6.47 g/100 g) in a liquid matrix with minimal fibre (0.11 g/100 g) causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; ADA guidelines classify all sugar-sweetened beverages as unsuitable for T2D management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid this product because its 6.47 g/100 g sugar in liquid form is rapidly absorbed, producing a high glycaemic response (estimated GI 65) with negligible fibre buffering; plain unsweetened hojicha without creamer or sugar is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer component likely contains palm-oil-derived saturated fat and the product is a sugar-sweetened beverage; both factors are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this product because the non-dairy creamer likely contributes palm-oil saturated fat and the added sugar (6.47 g/100 g) is associated with elevated triglycerides and LDL cholesterol; switching to plain hojicha brewed without creamer or sugar eliminates both risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content and palm-based saturated fat in non-dairy creamer are pro-inflammatory and negate the polyphenol benefit of hojicha
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product because the added sugar and palm-oil creamer promote systemic inflammation, outweighing the modest anti-inflammatory polyphenol content of the roasted tea; plain unsweetened hojicha is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 3-in-1 Hojicha Latte is an instant premixed beverage sachet containing roasted Japanese green tea (hojicha) powder, non-dairy creamer, and sugar, designed to be dissolved in hot water to produce a ready-to-drink latte-style tea. Hojicha is produced by roasting green tea leaves or stems at high temperature, which reduces caffeine and catechin content compared with standard green tea while imparting a characteristic toasty, caramel-like flavour with low bitterness. The 3-in-1 format combines all three components in a single sachet, making it a convenient instant beverage popular in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Per 100 g of prepared beverage, the product provides 55 kcal, 0.58 g protein, 1.76 g fat, 9.41 g carbohydrate (of which 6.47 g is sugar), 0.11 g dietary fibre, and 12 mg sodium. The relatively low calorie density reflects the dilute liquid matrix. Clinically, the product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 6.47 g/100 g sugar content, which exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold used in AHA and ADA guidelines. It is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The non-dairy creamer component likely contains palm-oil-derived saturated fat, adding a pro-inflammatory and atherogenic dimension. The estimated glycaemic index is 65 (medium), driven primarily by sucrose in a liquid matrix. Plain unsweetened hojicha brewed from loose powder or leaves is a clinically preferable alternative for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in instant hojicha latte?
Is hojicha latte suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of instant hojicha latte?
Is hojicha latte high in caffeine?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020006) at confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI component only) based on the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened instant tea drinks with creamer.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this instant 3-in-1 hojicha latte product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (D09020006) or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent product as of 2026. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the HPB Foodo database record. protein_g (0.58 g) and fat_g (1.76 g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. carbohydrate_g (9.41 g) is non-zero and sourced from HPB. No zero-macro justification is required for core macros as none are zero. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on the closest approved-source equivalent (instant sweetened tea drink with creamer and sugar) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. Glycaemic load per serving calculated for a 20 g sachet serving: GL = (65 × 1.882) / 100 = 12.2. Halal status assumed based on product category and typical formulation; consumers should verify product-specific halal certification. Vegan status is false due to likely casein-containing non-dairy creamer; consumers should verify ingredient label.
